279	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
280	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
281	
282	\begin{definition}
283	
284	\leanok
285	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
286	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
288	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
289	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
290	  \[
291	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
292	  \]
293	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
294	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
295	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
296	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
297	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
298	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
299	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
300	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
301	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
302	  canonical compatibility
303	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
304	     \;\cong\;
305	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
306	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
307	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
308	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
309	
310	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
311	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
312	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
313	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
314	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
315	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
316	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
317	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
318	\end{definition}
319	
320	\begin{lemma}
321	
322	\leanok
323	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
324	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
325	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
326	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
327	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
328	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
329	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
330	  \[
331	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
332	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
333	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
334	  \]
335	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
336	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
337	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
338	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
339	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
340	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
341	  \[
342	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
343	    \qquad
344	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
345	  \]
346	  the associator
347	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
348	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
349	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
350	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
351	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
352	\end{lemma}
353	
354	\begin{proof}
355	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
356	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
357	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
358	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
359	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
360	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
361	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
362	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
363	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
364	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
365	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
366	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
367	\end{proof}
368	
369	\begin{remark}
370	[The full monoidal category is cheap via route~(e); the group law consumes only propositions]
371	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
372	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
373	        lem:whisker_of_W}
374	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
375	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
376	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
377	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
378	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
379	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. Under route~(e) this is
380	  \emph{cheap}, not expensive: it is exactly the output of
381	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
382	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
383	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), once the single instance
384	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) is supplied. All the
385	  coherence is then derived by the API.
386	
387	  Crucially, \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) needs \emph{no} closed structure. Its
388	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
389	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
390	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
391	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
392	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\) (tensoring with an isomorphism
393	  preserves isomorphisms). This is the same technique Mathlib uses for
394	  fixed-base presheaves in
395	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\), ported to
396	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). It uses neither
397	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
398	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
399	  false over a non-affine open and is therefore off the critical path); the
400	  earlier reading that arbitrary-\(F\) whisker stability requires
401	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) was the \emph{wrong altitude}.
402	
403	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
404	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
405	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
406	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
407	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
408	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
409	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
410	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
411	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
412	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, all now supplied by the route~(e) monoidal structure;
413	  none of them is built from the off-path restriction-compatibility isomorphism
414	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
415	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
416	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
417	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\): there
418	  over a fixed ring, here over the sheafification localizer via
419	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\).
420	\end{remark}
421	
422	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
423	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
424	
425	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
426	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
427	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
428	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
429	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
430	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
431	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes ---
432	under route~(e) these are read off the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure of
433	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} --- together with the closure of \(\otimes\) on the
434	locally-trivial \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype
435	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and the interaction with the pullback
436	functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
437	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is retained as an off-path supplement, not as a
438	group-law ingredient; cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
439	
440	\begin{lemma}
441	
442	\leanok
443	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
444	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
445	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
446	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
447	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
448	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
449	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
450	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
451	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
452	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
453	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
454	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
455	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
456	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
457	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
458	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
459	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
460	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
461	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
462	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
463	  \[
464	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
465	      \;\;:=\;\;
466	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
467	  \]
468	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
469	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
470	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
471	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
472	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
473	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
474	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
475	\end{lemma}
476	
477	\begin{proof}
478	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
479	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
480	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
481	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
482	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
483	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
484	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
485	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
486	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
487	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
488	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
489	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
490	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
491	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
492	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
493	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
494	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
495	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
496	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
497	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
498	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
499	
500	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
501	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
502	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
503	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
504	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
505	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
506	\end{proof}
507	
508	\begin{lemma}
509	
510	\leanok
511	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
512	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
513	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
514	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
515	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
516	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
517	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
518	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
519	  morphism
520	  \[
521	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
522	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
523	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
524	  \]
525	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
526	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
527	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
528	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
529	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
530	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
531	\end{lemma}
532	
533	\begin{proof}
534	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
535	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
536	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
537	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
538	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
539	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
540	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
541	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
542	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
543	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
544	  isomorphism}
545	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
546	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
547	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
548	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
549	  therefore extension of scalars along
550	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
551	
552	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
553	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
554	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
555	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
556	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
557	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
558	  the abstract pullback functor.
559	
560	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
561	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
562	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
563	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
564	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
565	   \;\cong\;
566	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
567	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
568	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
569	  comparison
570	  \[
571	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
572	      \;\longrightarrow\;
573	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
574	      \;\otimes\;
575	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
576	  \]
577	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
578	
579	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
580	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
581	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
582	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
583	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
584	  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification
585	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) via
586	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
587	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
588	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
589	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
590	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
591	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
592	  coherence isomorphisms
593	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
594	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
595	  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.
596	\end{proof}
597	
598	\begin{lemma}
599	
600	\leanok
601	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
602	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
603	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
604	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
605	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
606	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
607	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
608	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
609	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
610	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
611	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
612	\end{lemma}
613	
614	\begin{proof}
615	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
616	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
617	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
618	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
619	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
620	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
621	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
622	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
623	\end{proof}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}
626	
627	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
628	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
629	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
630	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
631	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
632	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
633	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
634	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
635	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
636	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
637	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
638	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
639	  left-whiskered morphism
640	  \[
641	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
642	  \]
643	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
644	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
645	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
646	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
647	
648	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
649	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
650	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
651	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
652	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
653	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
654	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
655	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
656	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
657	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
658	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
659	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
660	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
661	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
662	\end{lemma}
663	
664	\begin{proof}
665	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
666	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
667	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
668	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
669	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
670	
671	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
672	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
673	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
674	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
675	  \[
676	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
677	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
678	  \]
679	
680	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
681	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
682	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
683	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
684	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
685	
686	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
687	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
688	  linear maps (Mathlib
689	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
690	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
691	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
692	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
693	
694	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
695	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
696	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
697	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
698	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
699	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
700	\end{proof}
701	
702	\begin{lemma}
703	
704	\leanok
705	[Sheafification inverts a localizer morphism of presheaves of modules]
706	  \label{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
707	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W}
708	  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
709	  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
710	  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
711	  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
712	  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
713	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
714	  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
715	  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
716	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
717	  isomorphism:
718	  \[
719	    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
720	    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}
721	  \]
722	  This is a closed go/no-go bridge --- it converts ``\,a morphism lies in
723	  \(J.W\)\,'' into ``\,its sheafification is invertible\,''. Under route~(e) the
724	  associator is the API-derived one (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), so this bridge is
725	  retained as a supplement rather than as the associator's load-bearing step.
726	\end{lemma}
727	
728	\begin{proof}
729	  This is a one-morphism reading of the structural identity that the
730	  sheafification of presheaves of \(R_0\)-modules \emph{is} the localization of
731	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R_0\) at the class
732	  \(J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0)\) of morphisms whose
733	  underlying map of presheaves of abelian groups is locally bijective. Concretely,
734	  the underlying \(\mathtt{AddCommGrp}\)-valued sheafification inverts exactly the
735	  locally bijective morphisms, and Mathlib's
736	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.inverseImage\_W\_toPresheaf\_eq\_inverseImage\_%
737	  isomorphisms}\) identifies the preimage of \(J.W\) under
738	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0\) with the preimage of the class of isomorphisms under
739	  the module-level sheafification functor. Reading that equality of morphism
740	  classes at the single morphism \(f\): the hypothesis
741	  \(J.W\,\bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\bigr)\) places \(f\) in
742	  that preimage, hence \((\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f\) is an
743	  isomorphism. No new construction is required beyond instantiating the existing
744	  Mathlib identity at one morphism; this lemma is a closed thin wrapper, retained
745	  as a supplement under route~(e).
746	\end{proof}

748	\section{Route~(e): the \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal structure}
749	\label{sec:tensorobj_route_e}
750	
751	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
752	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
753	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
754	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
755	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
756	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
757	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
758	whiskering-stability lemmas below.
759	
760	\paragraph{Two-tier strategy.} The group law is assembled by a \emph{two-tier}
761	strategy, of which this route~(e) is the second tier. The \textbf{primary} tier
762	builds the commutative group directly on isomorphism classes of \emph{locally
763	trivial} (invertible) sheaves (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring
764	Mathlib's ring-level Picard group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) /
765	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). On top of
766	the locally-trivial-scoped associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
767	\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} (all
768	either already proved or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled), it needs only the
769	restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
770	dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}; it uses \emph{no}
771	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
772	\(\mathtt{Skeleton}\), and in particular never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation
773	(d.2). The \textbf{fallback} tier is the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to
774	\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)
775	(this section); it is deferred because its load-bearing whiskering field
776	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, for an arbitrary whisker object, needs
777	(d.2) --- the stalk commuting with the relative tensor over a \emph{varying} ring, a
778	Mathlib-absent ingredient --- and is retained only for the full
779	monoidal generality on arbitrary modules, which the group law does not consume. The
780	load-bearing local-injectivity half of \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is thus the sole
781	remaining open obligation of the \emph{fallback} tier only.
782	
783	\begin{lemma}
784	
785	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
786	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
787	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap_germ, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap_bijective_of_isIso, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}
788	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
789	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
790	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
791	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
792	  \[
793	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
794	  \]
795	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
796	  characterised on germs by
797	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
798	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
799	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
800	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
801	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
802	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
803	
804	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
805	  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
806	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
807	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
808	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
809	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
810	\end{lemma}
811	
812	\begin{proof}
813	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
814	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
815	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
816	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
817	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
818	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
819	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
820	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
821	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
822	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
823	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
824	\end{proof}
825	
826	\begin{lemma}
827	
828	\leanok
829	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
830	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
831	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
832	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
833	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
834	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
835	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
836	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
837	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
838	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
839	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
840	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
841	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
842	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
843	  left-whiskered morphism
844	  \[
845	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
846	  \]
847	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
848	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
849	
850	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
851	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
852	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
853	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
854	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
855	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
856	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
857	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
858	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
859	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
860	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
861	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
862	\end{lemma}
863	
864	\begin{proof}
865	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
866	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
867	
868	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
869	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
870	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
871	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
872	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
873	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
874	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
875	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
876	  On such a \(V\):
877	  \begin{itemize}
878	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
879	      gives a canonical isomorphism
880	      \[
881	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
882	      \]
883	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
884	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
885	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
886	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
887	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
888	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
889	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
890	  \end{itemize}
891	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
892	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
893	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
894	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
895	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
896	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
897	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
898	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
899	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
900	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
901	
902	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
903	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
904	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
905	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
906	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
907	
908	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
909	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
910	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
911	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
912	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
913	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
914	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
915	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
916	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
917	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
918	  \[
919	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
920	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
921	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
922	  \]
923	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
924	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
925	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
926	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
927	
928	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
929	  pinned commit:
930	  \begin{enumerate}
931	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
932	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
933	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
934	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
935	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
936	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
937	      above.
938	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
939	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
940	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
941	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
942	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
943	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
944	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
945	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
946	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
947	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
948	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
949	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
950	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
951	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
952	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
953	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
954	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
955	  \end{enumerate}
956	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
957	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
958	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
959	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
960	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
961	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
962	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
963	
964	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
965	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
966	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
967	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
968	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
969	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
970	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
971	\end{proof}

973	\begin{lemma}
974	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
975	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
976	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_W, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_W}
977	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
978	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
979	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
980	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
981	  \(J.W\):
982	  \[
983	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
984	    \qquad
985	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
986	  \]
987	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
988	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
989	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
990	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
991	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
992	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
993	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
994	\end{lemma}
995	
996	\begin{proof}
997	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
998	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
999	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1000	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1001	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1002	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1003	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1004	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1005	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1006	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1007	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1008	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1009	\end{proof}

1215	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1216	  bundle. The dual
1217	  \[
1218	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1219	  \]
1220	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1221	  isomorphism
1222	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1223	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1224	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1225	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1226	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1227	\end{lemma}
1228	
1229	\begin{proof}
1230	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1231	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
1232	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
1233	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
1234	  The contraction map
1235	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
1236	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
1237	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
1238	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
1239	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
1240	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
1241	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
1242	\end{proof}
1243	
1244	\begin{lemma}
1245	
1246	\leanok
1247	[Closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial carrier
1248	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
1249	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
1251	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1252	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1253	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. The carrier is the
1254	  \emph{locally-trivial} subtype
1255	  \[
1256	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1257	    \;\;=\;\;
1258	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
1259	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}
1260	  \]
1261	  introduced in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. The bifunctor
1262	  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
1263	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
1264	  if \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M'\) then
1265	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\). Hence \(\otimes\)
1266	  closes on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\), supplying the binary
1267	  operation the relative group law multiplies with.
1268	\end{lemma}
1269	
1270	\begin{proof}
1271	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1272	  Closure is exactly
1273	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}: the tensor product of two
1274	  locally-trivial rank-one modules is again locally trivial of rank one
1275	  (\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_isLocallyTrivial}\)). The Lean construction
1276	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjOnProduct}\) applies this directly to a pair of objects of
1277	  the \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) subtype \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\),
1278	  returning the tensor with its locally-trivial witness; no
1279	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate and no coherence isomorphism enters this
1280	  closure statement. (The \(\otimes\)-invertibility carrier
1281	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} is a
1282	  separate predicate, connected to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) only by an
1283	  off-critical-path equivalence; the units-group packaging is
1284	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, not this lemma.)
1285	\end{proof}
1286	
1287	\begin{lemma}
1288	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1289	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1290	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1291	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1292	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1293	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1294	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1295	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1296	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1297	  functor
1298	  \[
1299	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1300	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1301	  \]
1302	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1303	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1304	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1305	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1306	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1307	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1308	  canonical isomorphism
1309	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1310	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1311	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1312	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{proof}
1316	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1317	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1318	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1319	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1320	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1321	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1322	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1323	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1324	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1325	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1326	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1327	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1328	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1329	  same cover.
1330	\end{proof}

1332	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1333	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1334	
1335	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1336	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1337	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1338	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1339	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1340	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1341	design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
1342	itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
1343	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
1344	critical path.
1345	
1346	\begin{definition}\leanok
1347	[\(\otimes\)-invertibility of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
1348	  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
1349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
1350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1351	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible

1353	  % (lemma-invertible, the "$\exists\,\mathcal{N}$" characterisation)
1354	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4079).
1355	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1356	  % "\begin{definition}
1357	  % \label{definition-invertible}

1364	  % \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
1365	  % $$
1366	  % is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
1367	  % {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1368	  % to $\mathcal{O}_X$.
1369	  % \end{definition}
1370	  % [\dots]
1371	  % \begin{lemma}

1378	  % such that

1385	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1386	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CS and Lemma~0B8K. An invertible
1387	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is one for which \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1388	  (-)\) is an equivalence; equivalently there exists \(\mathcal{N}\) with
1389	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and
1390	  then \(\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
1391	  \mathcal{O}_X)\).}
1392	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
1393	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1394	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
1395	  \[
1396	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
1397	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1398	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
1399	  \]
1400	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
1401	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
1402	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
1403	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
1404	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
1405	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
1406	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
1407	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
1408	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
1409	  \(\otimes_X\).

1412	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
1413	  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
1414	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
1415	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1416	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1417	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1418	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1419	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1420	  % modules), Definition 01CX (definition-pic) + Lemma lemma-constructions-
1421	  % invertible (tensor of invertibles is invertible; dual is the inverse)
1422	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200-L4213 and L4350-L4357).
1423	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1424	  % "\begin{lemma}

1427	  % \begin{enumerate}
1428	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{N}$ are invertible
1429	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then so is

1431	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
1432	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
1433	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1434	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$

1439	  % \begin{definition}

1446	  % \end{definition}"
1447	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1448	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1449	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1450	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1451	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1452	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1453	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1454	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1455	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1456	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1457	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1458	  01CR).
1459	
1460	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1461	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1462	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1463	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1464	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1465	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1466	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1467	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1468	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1469	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1470	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1471	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1472	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)). Associativity is the locally-trivial
1473	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors
1474	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1475	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each read as an equality of iso-classes ---
1476	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\) --- so that no pentagon, triangle or
1477	  hexagon coherence is ever consumed. This requires \emph{no}
1478	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and
1479	  \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\): the group is assembled from the
1480	  existence-of-isomorphism propositions alone, exactly as
1481	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) packages the invertible \(R\)-modules into a
1482	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) without any monoidal-category machinery. The pinned carrier
1483	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group structure;
1484	  the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the pinned
1485	  commit.
1486	
1487	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1488	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1489	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1490	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1491	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1492	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1493	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1494	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1495	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1496	
1497	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1498	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1499	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1500	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
1501	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
1502	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
1503	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
1504	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
1505	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
1506	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
1507	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
1508	  already supplies.
1509	\end{lemma}
1510	
1511	\begin{proof}
1512	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1513	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1514	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1515	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1516	  Work directly on isomorphism classes of locally-trivial sheaves, as in Mathlib's
1517	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1518	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
1519	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
1520	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1521	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
1522	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
1523	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
1524	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The three group laws are
1525	  the \emph{existence} of the coherence isomorphisms, read as equalities of
1526	  iso-classes: associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are
1527	  the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1528	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
1529	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
1530	  Finally every element is invertible: the dual \(L^{-1}\) of
1531	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong
1532	  \mathcal{O}_X\), so \([L^{-1}]\) is a two-sided inverse of \([L]\); equivalently,
1533	  \([L]\) is a unit precisely when \(L\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
1534	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried
1535	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). This assembles the abelian group ---
1536	  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}
1537	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
1538	  mirroring the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) that \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) carries on
1539	  iso-classes of invertible modules.
1540	
1541	  Alternatively (fallback), once \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} provides the full
1542	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) via
1543	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), the group is the units of the skeleton,
1544	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\), exactly the
1545	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
1546	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.
1547	\end{proof}

1594	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1595	  %  \end{dfn}"
1596	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
1597	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
1598	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1599	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1600	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1601	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1602	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1603	  \[
1604	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1605	  \]
1606	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1607	  abelian-group structure with addition
1608	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1609	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1610	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1611	  inverse
1612	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1613	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1614	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1615	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1616	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1617	  map
1618	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1619	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1620	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1621	\end{theorem}
1622	
1623	\begin{proof}
1624	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1625	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1626	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1627	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1628	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1629	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1630	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1631	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1632	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1633	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1634	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1635	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1636	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1637	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1638	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1639	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1640	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1641	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1642	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1643	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1644	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1645	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1646	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1647	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1648	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1649	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1650	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1651	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1652	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1653	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1654	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1655	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1656	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1657	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1658	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1659	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1660	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1661	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1662	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1663	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1664	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1665	
1666	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1667	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1668	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1669	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1670	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1671	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1672	
1673	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1674	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1675	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1676	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1677	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1678	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1679	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1680	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1681	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1682	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1683	\end{proof}
1684	
1685	\section{Out of scope}
1686	\label{sec:tensorobj_out_of_scope}
1687	
1688	The following are downstream of this chapter and live in dedicated
1689	sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
1690	\begin{itemize}
1691	  \item \textbf{The sheafified relative Picard functor and its abelian-group target}
1692	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
1693	              thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}) --- once
1694	    \cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj} is established, the
1695	    \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued presheaf, its \'etale sheafification, and
1696	    the sheafification preserving the abelian-group target close
1697	    against it (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}); none of those
1698	    declarations is the responsibility of this chapter.
1699	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
1700	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- consumes the
1701	    full \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued sheafified presheaf;
1702	    representability is downstream of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}.
1703	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3,
1704	    \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}) --- once representability is
1705	    in hand, the identity component is cut out by Hilbert polynomials;
1706	    that is independent of the tensor-product substrate of this chapter.
1707	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4,
1708	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) --- consumes the full Route~A stack
1709	    downstream of A.3.
1710	  \item \textbf{Mathlib upstream PR of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)} ---
1711	    the substrate of this chapter is project-side, intentionally; a
1712	    parallel Mathlib upstream PR is welcome but not on the critical
1713	    path. Once the substrate ships in Mathlib, the
1714	    \texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean} file can be slimmed to a single
1715	    import + namespace re-export, but no downstream Lean file changes.
1716	\end{itemize}
1717

1	\chapter{The relative Picard functor and its \'etale sheafification}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\). The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.

62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);

59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51

17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
